# Mohamed A. Hussein

> Columbia Business School
> Research areas: Persuasion, Political Marketing, Advertising, Language, Media, Reputation Management, Attitudes

## Papers

- [Fifteen Reasons You Should Read This Paper: How Providing Many Arguments Increases Perceptions of Both Expertise and Persuasive Intent](https://mohamedayhussein.github.io/papers/bergman-hussein-catapano-tormala-2025-many-arguments/): The common belief that 'more arguments = more persuasion' is wrong in many real-world contexts: piling on arguments can actually leave you no better off than giving just one or two.
- [When Is Political Advocacy Effective? Understanding the Goals and Actors of Political Advocacy](https://mohamedayhussein.github.io/papers/hussein-catapano-2025-political-advocacy/): The same political advocacy tactic can advance one goal while undermining another — for example, protests are effective at capturing media attention but tend to backfire for changing attitudes among skeptics.
- [How Do Consumers React to Ads That Meddle in Out-Party Primaries?](https://mohamedayhussein.github.io/papers/hussein-lee-wheeler-2025-out-party-primaries/): Voters strongly dislike candidates who meddle in the opposing party's primaries — even voters from the candidate's own party. Donations dropped by 35–60% in experiments when voters learned their candidate had used meddle ads.
- [Why Do People Choose Extreme Candidates? The Role of Identity Relevance](https://mohamedayhussein.github.io/papers/hussein-tormala-wheeler-2025-extreme-candidates/): People don't universally prefer extreme candidates — they prefer them when the political issue is personally identity-relevant (i.e., when their stance on it feels like a reflection of who they are). When the issue is not identity-relevant, the preference for extremity disappears or reverses.
- [You Versus We: How Pronoun Use Shapes Perceptions of Receptiveness](https://mohamedayhussein.github.io/papers/hussein-tormala-2024-pronoun-use/): Simply swapping 'you' for 'we' in a disagreement makes you more persuasive: saying 'we need to reconsider this' lands better than 'you need to reconsider this,' because 'you' pronouns feel accusatory and aggressive in adversarial contexts.
- [Reputational Costs of Receptiveness: When and Why Being Receptive to Opposing Political Views Backfires](https://mohamedayhussein.github.io/papers/hussein-wheeler-2024-reputational-costs/): Being open-minded to the opposing party's political views can hurt your reputation — rather than coming across as thoughtful or collaborative, receptive people are judged negatively by their own party because their open-mindedness looks like tolerating immorality.
- [Attitudinal Advocacy](https://mohamedayhussein.github.io/papers/hussein-tormala-2023-attitudinal-advocacy/): Attitude strength is the most reliable predictor of advocacy: the more certain and morally convicted people feel about their views, the more likely they are to share, persuade, donate, sign petitions, and take other action on behalf of those views.
- [Undermining Your Case to Enhance Your Impact: A Framework for Understanding the Effects of Acts of Receptiveness in Persuasion](https://mohamedayhussein.github.io/papers/hussein-tormala-2021-undermining-your-case/): Counterintuitively, saying 'I'm not entirely sure, but...' can make you more persuasive than projecting full confidence — but this only works when you already have credibility. For a novice or low-status source, expressing uncertainty backfires.
